personality_voice.book_baseline: Arthur is defined by a cluster of consistent, observable traits: genuine, almost childlike enthusiasm for Muggle objects and culture (asking Harry about plugs, parking meters, escalators, delighting in matches and ticket machines); warm, inclusive fatherliness toward Harry; a mild, slightly bumbling manner that conceals real moral seriousness; a tendency to be caught between his own enthusiasms and Molly's disapproval (the car, the toffees, the stitches); dry wit; and a capacity for genuine anger when his values are threatened (the Malfoy confrontation, the toffee incident). His speech is warm, slightly flustered, and full of eager questions. He is not passive — he argues with Molly about telling Harry the truth, he confronts Malfoy, he defends Winky, he challenges Diggory's treatment of the elf. He also shows quiet dignity under pressure (the Ministry scenes in OotP, the Deathly Hallows lift scene).
personality_voice.film_portrayal: The films preserve the core personality markers well: the Muggle enthusiasm is present in CoS (parking meter question, delight at the Grangers), PoA (the warning scene), GoF (matches, ticket machines, the Underground), OotP (the Ministry visit), and HBP (the workshop scene). The warmth toward Harry is consistent. The flustered-but-principled dynamic with Molly is present in CoS and DH. The moral seriousness surfaces in the GoF Dark Mark scene ('Barty! They're just kids') and the DH lift scene. The HBP workshop scene adds a quietly moving moment of paternal candor not in the books in that exact form, but consistent with his character.
personality_voice.difference: The films lose several specific speech textures: the extended, eager monologues about Muggle logistics (Portkeys, Apparition, the World Cup organization) are compressed or cut. The flustered self-correction pattern (the car, the toffees, the stitches) is partially preserved in CoS but largely absent thereafter. The dry humor and the pattern of being caught between enthusiasm and propriety is reduced. The Flourish and Blotts fight — in the books Arthur physically throws himself at Malfoy; in the film Hagrid physically restrains him before he can act, reducing his agency in that moment. His voice in the books is more verbally elaborate and self-interrupting; in the films he is more economical.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The core personality is recognizable and well-preserved. The Muggle enthusiasm, warmth, moral seriousness, and flustered-but-principled manner all survive. The penalty is for the loss of verbal texture — the eager, rambling explanatory speeches that define his voice — and for the softening of his physical assertiveness in the Malfoy confrontation. These are secondary traits, not core ones, so the penalty is moderate.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: Arthur serves multiple narrative functions: (1) conduit of Ministry intelligence to Harry and the reader (the Sirius Black warning, the Dark Mark aftermath, the Malfoy-Fudge connection, the Vanishing Cabinet investigation); (2) moral exemplar of the 'good wizard' who chooses Muggle-love and Order loyalty over career advancement; (3) active participant in Order operations; (4) protector and escort of Harry at key moments (the Ministry hearing in OotP); (5) a figure whose professional vulnerability (the inquiry in CoS, the fines, the underpowered office) illustrates the cost of integrity under a corrupt Ministry. He initiates the argument with Molly about telling Harry the truth, he actively investigates the Malfoy tip in HBP, he defends Winky against Diggory, he warns Harry about Black, he escorts Harry through the Ministry.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: The escort function (OotP Ministry visit) is well-preserved and is one of his strongest film scenes. The intelligence-conduit role is preserved in PoA (the warning) and HBP (the workshop scene, where he reports on the Vanishing Cabinet investigation). The moral-exemplar function is present throughout. The Order participation is shown in GoF and DH. However, several specific acts of agency are reduced or transferred: in the Flourish and Blotts scene, Hagrid physically intervenes before Arthur can act, whereas in the books Arthur initiates the physical confrontation himself. In the GoF Dark Mark scene, Arthur's active defense of the children and his methodical reasoning about Winky's innocence is compressed — the film gives him 'Barty! They're just kids' but removes his sustained logical argument. The HBP investigation of the Malfoy tip is handled in a private workshop scene rather than through the public Ministry channels described in the books, which reduces the sense of institutional agency.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The Flourish and Blotts fight is the clearest transfer: Arthur's physical initiative is given to Hagrid's restraining hand. The Winky defense scene loses Arthur's sustained logical argumentation. The CoS inquiry (which shows the cost of his integrity) is referenced but not dramatized. His role in DH is reduced to brief appearances.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The escort and intelligence-conduit functions are well-preserved. The penalty is for the transferred physical agency in CoS and the compressed reasoning agency in GoF. These are meaningful losses — the Malfoy fight is one of the clearest demonstrations of Arthur's capacity for passionate action — but the overall narrative role remains recognizable.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Arthur's motivations are consistent and clear: genuine love of Muggles and Muggle culture (not performative); commitment to a vision of wizarding society that includes rather than excludes; loyalty to Dumbledore and the Order even at professional cost; protective love for his family; and a recurring internal tension between his own enthusiasms/rule-bending (the enchanted car, the loophole in the law, the stitches experiment) and his role as a Ministry official who is supposed to uphold those rules. The PoA argument with Molly about telling Harry the truth is the clearest dramatization of his internal conflict: he believes Harry has a right to know and the capacity to handle it, against Molly's protectiveness.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The Muggle-love motivation is consistently present. The Order loyalty and family protection are present. The PoA warning scene preserves the core of his motivation (Harry is in danger, Harry deserves to know) but compresses the internal conflict — the overheard argument with Molly is cut entirely, and the film gives Arthur a direct, private conversation with Harry instead. This removes the tension between his judgment and Molly's, and removes the evidence that he had to fight for the right to tell Harry. The rule-bending/enthusiasm tension is present in CoS (the car, the loophole) but largely absent in later films.
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The most significant loss is the overheard PoA argument, which is the richest dramatization of Arthur's internal conflict in the entire series. In the film, he simply tells Harry — there is no evidence of the struggle to do so. The stitches subplot (OotP) is absent from the films, removing a comic but revealing instance of his Muggle enthusiasm overriding his better judgment.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: The motivations are correct but simplified. The Muggle-love and Order-loyalty motivations are intact. The internal conflict between his own judgment and Molly's, and between his enthusiasms and his official role, is present in CoS but largely flattened thereafter. The PoA argument is a meaningful loss — it is the scene that most clearly shows Arthur as someone who actively fights for what he believes is right, even within his own household.

character_arc.book_baseline: Arthur's arc across the series is relatively flat in terms of fundamental character change — he is consistently himself — but has meaningful developmental beats: the professional cost of his integrity (CoS inquiry, underpowered office); the near-death experience in OotP and recovery, which deepens the family's sense of mortality and his own quiet resilience; the promotion in HBP, which represents a belated institutional recognition of his worth; the Percy estrangement and reconciliation in DH; and his role in the final battle. The arc is less about transformation than about the sustained cost and eventual vindication of his values.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The CoS inquiry is referenced but not dramatized. The OotP snake attack and recovery are present and handled with reasonable fidelity — the hospital scenes, the stitches subplot is cut but the emotional core (family vigil, Harry's guilt, Arthur's cheerful resilience) is preserved in the Christmas scene. The HBP promotion is mentioned by Molly but Arthur himself does not appear to register it as significant. The Percy reconciliation in DH is present (a brief but emotionally effective moment). The final battle appearance is minimal.
character_arc.difference: The arc's key beats are mostly present but compressed. The professional-cost dimension (the inquiry, the underpowered office, Malfoy's contempt at the World Cup) is present but less sustained. The OotP recovery arc loses the stitches subplot, which is a small but characteristically Arthurian moment of resilience-through-curiosity. The HBP promotion is mentioned but not felt as a turning point.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc is compressed but the key beats survive. The flat-arc nature of the character means compression does less damage than it would for a character with a more transformative journey. The penalty is for the loss of the professional-cost dimension's sustained development and the stitches subplot.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Arthur's most important relationships are: (1) Molly — a partnership of equals with genuine tension, humor, and mutual respect; they argue, they worry together, they support each other; (2) Harry — a surrogate-father dynamic, with Arthur showing Harry the wizarding world with delight and protecting him with quiet determination; (3) Malfoy — a sustained class/values antagonism that reveals Arthur's dignity under contempt; (4) his children collectively — warm, slightly chaotic, occasionally exasperated fatherhood.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The Arthur-Molly dynamic is present but reduced — the films show them as a unit more than as two people in productive tension. The overheard PoA argument is the clearest loss. The Arthur-Harry relationship is well-handled in OotP (the Ministry visit is one of the best Arthur scenes in the films) and HBP (the workshop scene). The Malfoy antagonism is present in CoS and GoF but the sustained contempt dynamic across multiple books is compressed. The father-children relationship is present but given less texture.
key_relationships.difference: The Arthur-Molly tension is the most significant loss — the films present them as more uniformly aligned. The Arthur-Harry surrogate-father dynamic is well-preserved in OotP and HBP. The Malfoy relationship loses the sustained quality it has in the books.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The key relationships are mostly faithful in emotional tone but the Arthur-Molly dynamic loses its productive tension. The Arthur-Harry relationship is the strongest in the films and is well-preserved. The penalty is primarily for the flattening of the Molly dynamic.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Arthur's complexity comes from several sources: the contradiction between his official role (enforcing Muggle Artifact laws) and his personal enthusiasm for breaking them; his capacity for physical passion (the Malfoy fight) beneath a mild exterior; his quiet political courage (defending Winky, arguing with Molly, warning Harry against Ministry wishes); his professional humiliation (the underpowered office, the fines, Malfoy's contempt) borne with dignity; his genuine intellectual curiosity about Muggles that goes beyond charm into something almost scholarly; and the stitches subplot as a perfect encapsulation of his character — rule-breaking, curious, slightly embarrassed, resilient.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The contradiction between official role and personal enthusiasm is present but less sustained. The physical passion beneath the mild exterior is partially transferred to Hagrid in CoS. The political courage is present in the GoF Dark Mark scene and the OotP Ministry scenes. The professional humiliation is referenced but not dramatized with the same sustained texture. The stitches subplot is absent. The intellectual curiosity about Muggles is present but the extended, rambling explanatory speeches that give it texture are compressed.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The most significant losses are: (1) the stitches subplot — a perfect comic-and-revealing scene; (2) the sustained professional-humiliation texture; (3) the physical assertiveness in the Malfoy fight; (4) the overheard PoA argument as a demonstration of his moral courage within his own household; (5) the extended Muggle-curiosity speeches that give his enthusiasm intellectual depth rather than mere charm.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The films preserve the broad outline of Arthur's complexity but lose several of the specific scenes and patterns that give it texture. He remains recognizable and multi-dimensional, but the contradiction between rule-enforcer and rule-breaker, and the sustained dignity-under-humiliation dimension, are underserved. The stitches subplot is a meaningful absence — it is the scene that most perfectly captures all his contradictions at once.

lost_or_transferred_material: Arthur physically initiating the Malfoy fight in Flourish and Blotts — transferred to Hagrid's physical restraint
The overheard PoA argument with Molly about Harry's right to know about Sirius Black — cut entirely
Arthur's sustained logical defense of Winky at the Dark Mark scene — compressed to a single line
The stitches subplot in OotP — absent from the films
The extended Muggle-curiosity explanatory speeches (Portkeys, Apparition, World Cup logistics) — compressed
The CoS Ministry inquiry being dramatized as a consequence of the enchanted car
The underpowered, windowless office as a sustained symbol of professional humiliation
Arthur's loophole-in-the-law speech defending the enchanted car to Molly — partially preserved but shortened
Arthur's argument that Dumbledore's imprisonment would be catastrophic for the Order (OotP) — absent
score_caps_applied: Cap 3 (score above 75 if a core act of agency is transferred to another character) considered: the Malfoy fight transfer is meaningful but Arthur retains substantial agency elsewhere; cap applied as a ceiling check — final score of 74 falls just below 75, consistent with this transfer.
comparative.key_observations: Arthur Weasley is one of the better-preserved supporting characters in the film series. His core personality — Muggle enthusiasm, warm fatherliness, moral seriousness beneath a mild exterior — is consistently present across all eight films. The OotP Ministry visit and the HBP workshop scene are among the most faithful adaptations of any supporting character material in the series. The primary losses are: the overheard PoA argument (his richest moment of internal conflict), the physical initiative in the Malfoy fight (transferred to Hagrid), the stitches subplot (his most perfectly self-contradictory scene), and the sustained texture of his professional humiliation. These losses reduce his complexity without fundamentally misrepresenting him. The film Arthur is recognizably the book Arthur, but somewhat smoother — the productive tensions between his enthusiasms and his role, and between his judgment and Molly's, are underserved.
